topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email sat on my desk like a venomous snake. I stared at the screen, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. Every word—'unfortunately,' 'not selected'—felt like a deliberate insult. I slammed my fist onto the wooden surface, the sharp crack echoing in the otherwise silent dorm room. My breath hitched, a ragged, shallow sound escaping my chest. I reread the rejection letter, my eyes scanning the carefully chosen, yet now utterly meaningless, justifications. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space like a caged animal, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I needed to tear it up, to destroy the evidence of this profound unfairness.

[story 2]

"This is ridiculous." The words were a low growl, barely audible above the hum of the refrigerator. I picked up the printed notification, folding it once, twice, then crumpling the paper into a tight, furious ball. My hands trembled slightly, the anger vibrating through my fingers. I threw the crumpled mass against the wall, the sound surprisingly dull against the plaster. I walked over to the window, gripping the sill until my knuckles turned white. Outside, the world seemed too calm, too orderly, a stark contrast to the churning tempest inside my own head. I let out a long, frustrated sigh, the air tasting metallic in my mouth. I couldn't believe they had dismissed everything I had worked for.

[story 3]

I paced the length of the common room floor, my boots thudding a relentless rhythm. My gaze kept flicking back to the laptop screen, where the official denial glowed mockingly. My breathing was shallow, quick, as if I were physically struggling to draw enough air to sustain this level of agitation. I stopped abruptly, spinning around to face the empty room, my chest heaving. A sharp, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a harsh, humorless laugh. I clenched my fists so tightly that my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring, fueled by a burning sense of injustice. I wanted to scream until my voice gave out, to shake the very foundations of the institution that had just shut the door on me.
